title: US Federal Privacy Law Landscape — FTC, HIPAA, GLBA, COPPA, FERPA, FCRA
official_title: Overview of US Federal Privacy and Data Security Laws
overview: The United States has no comprehensive federal privacy law. Instead, privacy regulation is sector-specific, regulated by a patchwork of laws covering healthcare, finance, children, education, and consumer credit. The FTC Act provides a backstop enforcement mechanism against deceptive and unfair privacy practices. Key federal laws are described below. Congress has repeatedly attempted to pass a comprehensive federal privacy law (most recently the American Privacy Rights Act in 2024) without success.

federal_privacy_laws.scope: All for-profit entities in or affecting US commerce (with common carrier and non-profit exceptions)
key_provisions: Prohibits unfair or deceptive acts or practices
FTC uses this authority to enforce against privacy policy violations, inadequate security, deceptive data practices
Section 5(n): Unfair practice must cause substantial consumer injury, not reasonably avoidable, not outweighed by benefits
FTC can issue orders, seek civil penalties for violations of orders ($51,744 per violation per day)
federal_privacy_laws.ai_relevance: FTC has taken action against algorithmic discrimination, AI fraud, and deceptive AI practices. 2023 Policy Statement applies Section 5 to AI. FTC v. Amazon (Alexa children's privacy), FTC v. Rite Aid (facial recognition).

federal_privacy_laws.law: Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA)
federal_privacy_laws.scope: Covered entities (healthcare providers, health plans, healthcare clearinghouses) and their business associates
key_provisions: Privacy Rule: Standards for protected health information (PHI) use and disclosure
Security Rule: Administrative, physical, and technical safeguards for electronic PHI
Breach Notification Rule: Notify individuals, HHS, and media within 60 days of breach discovery
Minimum necessary standard: Disclose only minimum necessary PHI
Individual rights: Access, amendment, accounting of disclosures
federal_privacy_laws.penalty_structure: Tiered: $100–$50,000 per violation (up to $1.9M per violation category per year). Criminal penalties up to $250,000 and 10 years imprisonment.
federal_privacy_laws.ai_relevance: AI systems processing PHI are subject to HIPAA. Clinical decision support software may be covered. HHS issued guidance on HIPAA and AI/ML tools.

federal_privacy_laws.regulator: FTC, CFPB, banking regulators (OCC, Fed, FDIC, NCUA)
federal_privacy_laws.scope: Financial institutions — broadly defined to include any company significantly engaged in financial activities
key_provisions: Privacy Rule: Annual privacy notices to customers about sharing practices
Opt-out right: Customers can opt out of sharing with non-affiliated third parties
Safeguards Rule (amended 2021): Comprehensive information security programs required. Specific technical safeguards (MFA, encryption, access controls, penetration testing)
Pretexting prohibition: Ban on fraudulent access to customer financial information
federal_privacy_laws.penalty_structure: FTC: Civil penalties up to $100,000 per violation. Officers/directors: up to $10,000 per violation and imprisonment. Banking regulators have additional authorities.
federal_privacy_laws.ai_relevance: AI-driven lending, credit scoring, and financial products subject to GLBA. Safeguards Rule's technical requirements apply to AI systems handling financial data.

federal_privacy_laws.scope: Operators of websites or online services directed to children under 13, or with actual knowledge they collect data from children under 13
key_provisions: Verifiable parental consent required before collecting personal information from children under 13
Privacy notice requirements — link on homepage
Parents' rights: access, delete, refuse further collection of child's data
Data retention limits: retain only as long as reasonably necessary
Reasonable security measures required
2024 COPPA 2.0 proposal would expand to cover 13–17 year olds
federal_privacy_laws.penalty_structure: Up to $51,744 per violation per day. FTC has assessed penalties exceeding $100M (Google/YouTube $170M, TikTok $5.7M, Epic Games $275M).
federal_privacy_laws.ai_relevance: AI-powered apps, games, and services used by children subject to COPPA. Generative AI services with child users face heightened scrutiny.

federal_privacy_laws.law: Family Educational Rights and Privacy Act (FERPA)
federal_privacy_laws.scope: Educational agencies and institutions receiving federal funding
key_provisions: Students/parents have right to access education records
Schools cannot disclose education records without consent (with exceptions)
Directory information may be disclosed without consent unless opt-out exercised
Records of law enforcement, employment, alumni, treatment records excluded
federal_privacy_laws.penalty_structure: No private right of action. DOE can terminate federal funding (nuclear option — rarely used). Complaint-based enforcement.
federal_privacy_laws.ai_relevance: EdTech AI tools processing student data subject to FERPA. AI-based assessment and proctoring tools face scrutiny.

federal_privacy_laws.scope: Consumer reporting agencies and users of consumer reports
key_provisions: Consumers have right to access and dispute credit reports
Users of consumer reports must have permissible purpose
Adverse action notice required when using consumer report to make adverse decisions (credit, employment, housing)
Accuracy requirements: reasonable procedures to ensure maximum possible accuracy
Disposal rule: proper disposal of information derived from consumer reports
federal_privacy_laws.penalty_structure: Willful violations: actual damages or statutory damages $100–$1,000 per consumer; punitive damages. Negligent violations: actual damages only.
federal_privacy_laws.ai_relevance: AI credit scoring and algorithmic underwriting subject to FCRA. CFPB has stated that AI 'black box' models cannot satisfy adverse action notice requirements — specific reasons must be provided.

ftc_privacy_enforcement_trends: Increased focus on dark patterns and manipulative design
AI and biometric data — facial recognition enforcement (Rite Aid, Clearview AI)
Health data — enforcement against non-HIPAA health apps using tracking pixels
Algorithmic harm — 2023 Policy Statement on enforcement against AI discrimination

pending_federal_legislation.american_privacy_rights_act: APRA introduced 2024 — bipartisan comprehensive federal privacy bill. Would preempt most state laws, create private right of action, require data minimisation, targeted advertising opt-out, and algorithm transparency. Did not pass in 119th Congress.
pending_federal_legislation.kids_online_safety_act: KOSA passed Senate in 2024 — duty of care for minors online platforms. Status uncertain.
pending_federal_legislation.american_data_privacy_protection_act: Prior comprehensive bill. Did not pass.

penalty_structure.ftc_section_5_orders: Civil penalties up to $51,744/day/violation for violations of FTC orders
penalty_structure.hipaa: Up to $1.9M per violation category per year
penalty_structure.coppa: Up to $51,744 per violation per day
penalty_structure.fcra_willful: $100–$1,000 per consumer per violation plus punitive damages

cross_references.relevance: California has the most comprehensive state law. Federal sectoral laws (HIPAA, GLBA, FCRA) preempt CCPA in their domains — regulated entities need to understand the interplay.
US companies doing business in EU must comply with GDPR regardless of US law. US-EU Data Privacy Framework provides transfer mechanism for US companies that certify.
FTC enforcement powers extend to AI practices. Section 5 applied to AI discrimination, deceptive AI claims, and AI-enabled dark patterns. CFPB has issued guidance on AI credit decisions.
